title: GE Aerospace (GE) Intrinsic Value — Opus 4.6 Analysis
description: Opus 4.6 independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of GE Aerospace (GE).

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y10)
fcfGrowth.rationale: GE Aerospace is projected to grow FCF at 12% annually, reflecting its competitive position, sector dynamics, and historical compounding rate.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: A 9.5% WACC balances GE Aerospace's earnings quality and balance sheet strength against sector volatility and macroeconomic risk factors.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 3.0% terminal rate assumes long-run growth converges toward nominal GDP, appropriate for a mature S&P 500 constituent.

thesisParagraphs: GE Aerospace (GE) is a large-cap constituent of the S&P 500 with established market position and consistent cash flow generation. The company's competitive advantages and market share provide a degree of pricing power and earnings stability that supports a premium valuation.

My DCF applies a 12% FCF growth rate reflecting the company's historical compounding ability and forward outlook, with a 9.5% discount rate capturing sector-specific risks and capital market conditions. The valuation is sensitive to assumptions about sustainable growth — investors should stress-test these inputs against their own convictions.
verdictFooter: Opus 4.6 independent analysis. Data via public filings (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: What is GE Aerospace's intrinsic value according to Opus?
faq.answer: Based on a 10-year DCF with 12% FCF growth and 9.5% WACC, Opus calculates GE's intrinsic value at approximately $186.09 per share, suggesting the stock is fairly valued at $200.00.

faq.question: What assumptions drive GE's valuation?
faq.answer: The key drivers are the FCF growth rate (12%) and discount rate (9.5%). A 1% increase in growth adds roughly 10-15% to the intrinsic value. These assumptions reflect Opus's independent assessment of the company's competitive position and risk profile.

faq.question: Should I buy or sell based on this analysis?
faq.answer: This is not financial advice. DCF models are inherently subjective — small changes in assumptions create large swings in output. Use this as one data point among many in your investment research process.
